meta.theme: 미드·영화에서 자주 나오는 진짜 미국 영어

english_theme_packs.theme_title_en: Expressions You'll Hear in Every American TV Show

expressions.expression: You've got to be kidding me.
expressions.meaning: 농담하는 거죠? 말도 안 돼요. 믿기 어려운 상황에 터져 나오는 반응.
expressions.nuance: 미드 전 장르를 통틀어 가장 자주 나오는 표현 중 하나입니다. 분노·충격·황당함·황폐함을 모두 담을 수 있는 만능 표현이에요. 톤에 따라 뜻이 크게 달라집니다 — 살짝 웃으며 쓰면 '설마요?', 딱딱하게 쓰면 '진짜 말도 안 된다'는 뜻이 됩니다. 'You're kidding!'이나 'No way!'와 함께 쓰면 더 자연스럽습니다.
expressions.dialogue:
A: The launch is postponed. Again. Third time this year.
B: You've got to be kidding me. What happened this time?
A: Some compliance issue they found last minute.
B: I can't believe this. The whole team has been working nights for this.
expressions.example: You've got to be kidding me — the flight got canceled and they want to put me on a flight two days later?
expressions.practice: You've got to be kidding me. We spent three months on this proposal and they went with someone else without even telling us?

expressions.meaning: 지금 진심이에요? 지금 그 말이 진짜예요? 믿기 힘든 말을 들었을 때 확인하는 표현.
expressions.nuance: 'Are you serious?'보다 'right now'가 붙으면 현재 상황에 대한 당혹감이 더해집니다. 'Seriously?'를 한 단어로 줄여 쓰기도 합니다. 미드에서 갈등·반전·충격적인 폭로 장면에서 거의 빠지지 않고 등장합니다. 감탄사처럼 'Seriously?' 한 마디만 쓰는 경우도 매우 흔합니다.
expressions.dialogue:
A: They want all the revisions done by tomorrow morning.
B: Are you serious right now? It's already 7pm.
A: I know. They said it's non-negotiable.
B: Then someone needs to tell them what 'non-negotiable' actually costs.
expressions.example: Are you serious right now? You're telling me I've been CCed on every email for six months and nobody noticed I wasn't actually in this department?
expressions.practice: Are you serious right now? You knew about the issue for two weeks and didn't mention it until the day before the client presentation?

expressions.meaning: 바로 이거예요! 이게 제가 말하던 거예요! 기대하던 것이 드디어 나왔을 때 터지는 표현.
expressions.nuance: 뭔가 잘 됐을 때, 좋은 일이 생겼을 때, 자신이 주장하던 것이 맞았다고 드러났을 때 쓰는 기쁨의 표현입니다. 'Yes! That's exactly what I meant!'를 캐주얼하게 압축한 것입니다. 스포츠 중계, 팀 성과 발표, 친구 사이의 좋은 소식 공유 등에서 매우 자주 등장합니다.
expressions.dialogue:
A: The numbers are in — we hit 130% of our quarterly target.
B: That's what I'm talking about! I knew if we focused on the enterprise segment we'd see this.
A: You called it three months ago.
B: Now let's make sure this becomes the baseline, not the exception.
expressions.example: You stayed up all night and fixed the entire pipeline? That's what I'm talking about — this is the kind of ownership that makes a team great.
expressions.practice: Finally, a design that feels both premium and accessible. That's what I'm talking about — this is the direction we needed.

expressions.meaning: 저도 그런 경험 있어요. 상대방의 힘든 상황에 공감하며 '나도 겪어봤다'고 말할 때.
expressions.nuance: 직역하면 '나 거기 가본 적 있어'지만, 실제 의미는 '그 상황을 나도 겪어봤어요'입니다. 위로나 공감을 표현할 때 'I understand'나 'I know how you feel'보다 훨씬 생생하고 진심 어린 표현입니다. 뒤에 자신의 경험을 짧게 덧붙이면 더욱 자연스럽습니다.
expressions.dialogue:
A: I feel like no matter how hard I work, I'm not getting recognized. It's demoralizing.
B: I've been there. In my last job I put in 18 months of serious work before anyone really noticed. What got me through was keeping a record of my wins — even the small ones.
A: That's a good idea actually.
B: When you start doubting yourself, the evidence helps.
expressions.example: I've been there — the burnout, the feeling that no one sees what you're contributing. It does get better.
expressions.practice: I've been there with the imposter syndrome. The first six months in a new role, I questioned every decision I made.

expressions.meaning: 제 개인적인 의견인데요, 참고로 제 생각을 말하자면. 의견을 부드럽게 꺼낼 때 쓰는 표현.
expressions.nuance: 2센트(매우 작은 금액)짜리 의견이라는 겸손한 표현입니다. '제 생각이 맞을 수도 있고 아닐 수도 있지만, 그래도 한마디 보태자면'이라는 뉘앙스입니다. 회의나 토론에서 자신의 의견을 강요하지 않으면서도 말하고 싶을 때 씁니다. 'Just my two cents'처럼 'just'를 앞에 붙여 더 겸손하게 만들 수 있습니다.
expressions.dialogue:
Manager: We're still deciding between the two vendors. Any thoughts?
You: Just my two cents — I'd look at their post-sale support record more than the initial price. We learned that the hard way last year.
Manager: That's a fair point.
You: Take it or leave it, obviously — just wanted to flag it.
expressions.example: For what it's worth, my two cents: the product is great but the onboarding is losing people in the first ten minutes.
expressions.practice: My two cents — I think we're optimizing the wrong thing. We're focused on acquisition when retention is the actual problem.

master_paragraph.text: You've got to be kidding me — you're telling me nobody flagged this until now? Are you serious right now? Okay, deep breath. I've been there — I've had projects blow up in the exact same way, and there's always a way through. My two cents: we stop pointing fingers and focus on what we can still fix before the client sees it. And when this is resolved — and it will be — that's what I'm talking about. Let's turn this into the moment the team proved what it's made of.
master_paragraph.translation: 말도 안 돼 — 지금까지 아무도 이걸 몰랐다고요? 지금 진심으로 하는 말이에요? 좋아요, 심호흡. 저도 그런 경험 있어요 — 똑같은 방식으로 프로젝트가 터진 적 있어요, 그리고 항상 방법은 있었어요. 제 의견을 말하자면: 손가락질 멈추고 클라이언트가 보기 전에 고칠 수 있는 것에 집중해요. 그리고 이게 해결되면 — 반드시 됩니다 — 바로 그게 제가 말하는 거예요. 이 순간을 팀이 진가를 보여준 순간으로 만들어봅시다.
highlights: You've got to be kidding me

english_expressions.meaning: 말도 안 돼!, 진짜요? (놀람·믿기 어려움을 표현. 실제로 나가라는 뜻이 아님)
english_expressions.example: You got the job at Google? Get out of here — that's incredible!
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: I ran into the CEO at the coffee shop and we ended up talking for 45 minutes. He wants to schedule a proper meeting.
B: Get out of here! How did that even happen?
A: I was sitting next to him and recognized him from a podcast interview.
B: That's unbelievable. What did you talk about?

english_expressions.meaning: 에이 진짜! / 어서요! / 제발요! (상황에 따라 격려·불만·재촉·설득 모두 가능)
english_expressions.example: Come on, you can do this — you've prepared for this presentation for two weeks.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: I don't think I should apply. I'm probably not qualified enough.
B: Come on! You have five years of experience and you know this industry better than most. Just apply.
A: But the job description says—
B: Job descriptions are wishlists. Come on. Apply.

english_expressions.meaning: 별거 아니에요, 대단한 일도 아니에요. 상대방이 과도하게 걱정하거나 사과할 때 진정시키는 표현.
english_expressions.example:
A: I'm so sorry I sent the wrong file.
B: Honestly, it's not a big deal — I hadn't opened it yet.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: I feel terrible about missing the team dinner.
B: It's really not a big deal. These things happen. We'll do another one next month.
A: I should have let you know earlier.
B: You sent a message as soon as you knew. That's all anyone can ask.

english_expressions.meaning: 드디어!, 이제야!, 때가 됐죠! 오래 기다리던 것이 마침내 일어났을 때.
english_expressions.example: They finally promoted her to director? It's about time — she's been doing director-level work for two years.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: We finally got approval for the budget increase.
B: It's about time! We submitted that request four months ago.
A: I know. Better late than never.
B: Now let's actually use it before they change their minds.

english_expressions.meaning: 해봐요, 어서 해요, 도전해봐요. 누군가의 결심이나 도전을 지지할 때.
english_expressions.example: You're thinking about starting your own business? Go for it — you've been talking about this for years.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: I want to ask for a raise but I'm not sure if the timing is right.
B: You've crushed every target for three quarters straight. If not now, when? Go for it.
A: What if they say no?
B: Then you ask what it would take, and you have a clear target. You win either way.

english_expressions.meaning: 조금만 더 버텨요, 힘내요. 힘든 상황을 견디고 있는 사람에게 건네는 응원.
english_expressions.example: The first month in a new role is always the hardest — hang in there.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: I'm exhausted. The project has been brutal and there's no end in sight.
B: Hang in there. I know it doesn't feel like it, but you're almost through the hard part.
A: How can you tell?
B: Because the chaos is starting to have a pattern now. That means it's getting manageable.

english_expressions.meaning: 진짜요? 설마요? 믿기 어렵거나 황당한 상황에 내뱉는 한마디.
english_expressions.example:
A: They moved the deadline up by a week. Again.
B: Seriously?
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: The client wants us to redo the entire branding direction two days before the final presentation.
B: Seriously? After we incorporated all their previous feedback?
A: I know.
B: Okay. Seriously, though — we need to get on a call with them today and manage this before it becomes a full rewrite.

english_expressions.meaning: 그거야 그렇죠, 맞는 말이에요, 납득할 수 있어요. 상대방의 논리를 인정할 때.
english_expressions.example:
A: I can't stay late tonight — I have a commitment I can't cancel.
B: Fair enough. Can you get me the draft first thing tomorrow?
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: I think we should push back on the deadline. We can't do quality work under this timeline.
B: Fair enough — let me take it to the client and see what flexibility they have.
A: If they say no, we can still discuss what scope to cut.
B: Good thinking. I'll frame it as a quality conversation, not a capacity problem.

english_expressions.meaning: 그만해요, 그만 좀 해요. 상대방의 행동이나 말을 중단시키고 싶을 때.
english_expressions.example: Cut it out — we've been going back and forth on this for an hour. Let's just make a decision.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: Oh come on, you always do this — you never commit to a direction.
B: Cut it out. That's not fair and you know it. Let's focus on the actual problem.
A: You're right. I'm sorry. I'm just frustrated.
B: So am I. Let's channel that into solving this.

english_expressions.meaning: 무슨 일이에요? 어떻게 된 거예요? 상황을 파악하거나 안부를 물을 때.
english_expressions.example: You look stressed — what's going on?
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: Hey, what's going on? I heard there was some tension in the team meeting.
B: It got a bit heated. Two people have completely different visions for where the product should go.
A: Has it been building for a while?
B: Yeah, this was the first time it came out in the open though.

english_expressions.meaning: 잘했어요! 대단해요! 누군가의 성과나 행동을 칭찬할 때.
english_expressions.example: You negotiated the contract down by 20%? Way to go!
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: I finally finished the certification exam. I passed.
B: Way to go! How long did you study for that?
A: Three months, every weekend.
B: It shows. You should be really proud.

english_expressions.meaning: 나 이제 못 하겠어, 더 이상 안 돼. 지치거나 포기하고 싶은 감정을 표현할 때.
english_expressions.example: Three all-nighters in a row and they've just added more scope. I'm done.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: They just pushed back the launch date and doubled the deliverables.
B: I'm done. I genuinely cannot keep doing this.
A: I hear you. Let's write down everything that's piled up and bring it to management tomorrow.
B: If nothing changes, I'm seriously considering my options.
A: That's fair. But let's have the conversation first.

english_expressions.meaning: 다 털어놔봐요, 얘기해봐요. 흥미로운 이야기나 비밀을 털어놓으라고 재촉할 때.
english_expressions.example: You said you had news about the merger — spill it!
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: Okay, I have to tell you something but you cannot repeat it.
B: Oh my god. Spill.
A: Apparently they're restructuring the whole product team.
B: Are you serious? Where did you hear this?
A: Direct from someone on the exec team.

english_expressions.meaning: 좀 봐줘요, 말도 안 돼요, 제발요. 불합리한 상황이나 상대방의 말에 불만을 표현할 때.
english_expressions.example: Give me a break — you can't expect the entire report rewritten by tomorrow morning.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: They want you to work through the weekend again.
B: Give me a break. This is the fourth weekend in a row.
A: I know. Is there any part of it you can push back on?
B: I'll tell them I can do half of it and we need to discuss timelines properly.

english_expressions.meaning: 맞는 말이에요, 한 방 먹었네요. 상대방의 날카로운 지적이나 반론을 인정할 때.
english_expressions.example:
A: You said we should always prioritize data over intuition, but you just made that call on gut feeling.
B: Touché.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: You keep saying we should do more with less, but every new initiative you introduce adds workload.
B: Touché. That's a legitimate point. I need to be more deliberate about what we stop doing before I propose what to start.
A: I wasn't trying to be critical.
B: No, it's a fair observation. Let's actually make a 'stop doing' list.

english_expressions.meaning: 그럴 줄 알았어, 역시나, 예상했던 일이네. 예상대로 나쁜 일이 생겼을 때.
english_expressions.example: They changed the requirements the week before launch? That figures.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: The vendor's raising their rates again.
B: That figures. They do this every time we get locked into a multi-year contract.
A: So what's the play?
B: We start exploring alternatives now, before renewal. Let's not be in this position again.

english_expressions.meaning: 왜 그래요? 뭐가 문제예요? 상대방의 이상한 행동이나 태도를 이해 못 할 때.
english_expressions.example: You've been short with everyone all morning — what's your deal?
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: What's your deal today? You've been dismissing every idea before anyone finishes their sentence.
B: Sorry, I got some bad news this morning and I'm letting it bleed into work.
A: Do you need a few minutes?
B: No, I'm okay. Let me reset. Go ahead with the proposal.

english_expressions.meaning: (웃겨서/황당해서) 죽겠어요, 너무 웃기다. 매우 웃기거나 황당한 상황에 쓰는 캐주얼 표현.
english_expressions.example:
A: He showed up to the all-hands in a full suit while everyone else was in hoodies.
B: I'm dead.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: Did you see the email the CEO accidentally sent to the whole company instead of just his assistant?
B: I'm dying — he literally described every board member by their worst habit.
A: Apparently he's been working on it for months.
B: That's the best accidental transparency I've ever seen.

english_expressions.meaning: 어떻게 됐어요? 잘 됐어요? 뭔가를 한 뒤 결과를 물어볼 때.
english_expressions.example: How did the client call go? I've been thinking about you all morning.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: How did the pitch go?
B: Better than I expected, honestly. They asked a lot of tough questions but I think that means they're actually interested.
A: What was the vibe at the end?
B: They said they'd follow up by Thursday. When they give a specific day, that's usually a good sign.

english_expressions.meaning: ~는 어떻게 된 거예요? ~가 왜 그래요? 상황이나 행동이 이해가 안 될 때 물을 때.
english_expressions.example: What's the deal with the new approval process? It used to take a day, now it takes two weeks.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: What's the deal with the new seating policy? Nobody's been told anything officially.
B: From what I heard, they're trying to encourage cross-team collaboration by mixing departments.
A: That would've been nice to know before everyone started getting territorial.
B: Agree. The communication on this one has been poor.

english_expressions.meaning: 저도 같은 생각이에요, 동의해요. 상대방의 의견에 강하게 동의할 때.
english_expressions.example: I'm completely with you on that — we've been spreading resources too thin for too long.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: I think we're overcomplicating this. The simplest solution is usually the right one.
B: I'm with you on that a hundred percent. We keep adding layers trying to look thorough, but we're just creating confusion.
A: Exactly. Let's strip this back to three core points.
B: That's the deck I want to present.

english_expressions.meaning: 어때요? 어떻게 생각해요? 제안을 하고 난 뒤 상대방의 의사를 물을 때.
english_expressions.example: I'll buy lunch, you handle the afternoon standup. What do you say?
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: How about this — I draft the proposal, you handle the client relationship side, and we split the commission. What do you say?
B: I'd want to see the scope of work first before I agree to a split.
A: Fair. I can have a first draft to you by Wednesday.
B: Then let's revisit Thursday. If the scope works, I'm in.

english_expressions.meaning: ~라고 했을 때 이미 설득됐어요. 상대방이 특정 말을 했을 때부터 마음이 돌아섰다는 표현.
english_expressions.example: You had me at 'four-day work week.'
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: The new role comes with full flexibility — you set your own hours, fully remote, and you pick your own team.
B: You had me at 'fully remote.' Tell me more.
A: And the comp package is—
B: Honestly, after remote and own hours, whatever it is, I'm listening.
english_expressions.dialogue_note: 영화 Jerry Maguire(1996)의 'You had me at hello'에서 유래. 현재는 일상 대화에서 폭넓게 쓰임.

english_expressions.meaning: 제가 처리할게요, 나한테 맡겨요. 자신감 있게 책임지겠다고 선언할 때.
english_expressions.example: Don't worry about the client call — I've got this.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: Are you sure you're ready to run this meeting solo?
B: I've got this. I've been in every planning session and I know this material better than anyone.
A: Okay. I'm here if you need backup.
B: Appreciate it. But watch — I've got it covered.

english_expressions.meaning: 부탁 하나 들어줘요. 'Do me a favor'의 더 캐주얼한 버전.
english_expressions.example: Hey, do me a solid and don't mention this to the director before I've had a chance to explain the context.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: Can you do me a solid?
B: Depends what it is. Last time you said that you needed me to pretend I'd seen a report I hadn't.
A: This is way simpler. Just cc me on the vendor emails so I'm in the loop.
B: That I can do.

english_expressions.meaning: 진짜 진심으로요, 농담 아니에요. 자신의 말이 완전히 진지하다는 것을 강조할 때.
english_expressions.example: I'm dead serious — if we don't fix the onboarding flow this quarter, we're going to lose 40% of signups before they ever see the product.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: You think we should scrap the whole roadmap and start over?
B: I'm dead serious. I know it sounds drastic but we've been building on a broken foundation for two years.
A: That's going to be a hard sell to leadership.
B: It'll be a harder sell when the product fails in eighteen months.

english_expressions.meaning: 별거 아니에요, 대단한 일 아니에요. 칭찬이나 감사를 겸손하게 받을 때, 또는 상황을 축소할 때.
english_expressions.example:
A: Thank you so much for covering for me yesterday.
B: No big deal — I was happy to help.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: You handled that client crisis completely on your own while I was traveling. That was impressive.
B: No big deal — I just followed the playbook we built together and used some common sense.
A: Don't undersell it. You made three good judgment calls under pressure.
B: Okay, maybe a little big deal.

english_expressions.meaning: 그러게 말이에요, 나한테 그 말을 해요? 나도 이미 알고 있다는 강한 공감의 표현.
english_expressions.example:
A: The commute today was absolutely brutal.
B: You're telling me — I was on the subway for an hour and a half.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: This client is impossible to please. Every time we make a change, they want something different.
B: You're telling me. I've been on this account for six months and I don't think they've approved a single deliverable without requesting revisions.
A: At some point someone needs to have a frank conversation with them.
B: I think that point is now.

english_expressions.meaning: 많을수록 좋죠. 사람이나 무언가가 더 많이 올수록 좋다는 환영의 표현.
english_expressions.example:
A: Can I bring two colleagues to the workshop?
B: The more the merrier — the more perspectives, the better.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: Two more people from the data team want to join the brainstorm.
B: The more the merrier. The problem we're solving touches their work directly anyway.
A: I'll send them the brief so they come prepared.
B: Good call. Prepared people make for better sessions.

english_expressions.meaning: 뭐 하고 있어요? 요즘 어때요? 가볍게 근황을 물을 때 쓰는 일상 표현.
english_expressions.example:
A: Hey, haven't seen you in a while — what are you up to these days?
B: Mostly heads-down on the new product launch.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: What are you up to this weekend?
B: Nothing major — I need to do some actual downtime. It's been a brutal month.
A: Same. I was thinking of a short hike Saturday morning if you want to join.
B: That sounds exactly like what I need.
